sections.title: Part 1: Understanding AI and System Limitations

questions.scenario: A colleague says, "ChatGPT is basically a search engine like Google." What is the most accurate response?

options.label: A
options.text: That's correct - both return information from the web

options.label: B
options.text: Not quite - LLMs generate responses by predicting the next word based on patterns in training data, whereas search engines index and retrieve existing web pages

options.label: C
options.text: They're the same because they both use AI

questions.rationale: LLMs are generative models, not retrieval engines. Understanding this distinction helps teachers set appropriate expectations for AI output.

questions.scenario: You ask an AI tool to provide key dates for a history lesson. It confidently lists five dates, but two of them are wrong. This is an example of:

options.label: A
options.text: A software bug that will be fixed in the next update

options.label: B
options.text: The AI deliberately giving incorrect information

options.label: C
options.text: An AI "hallucination" - where the model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect content

questions.rationale: Hallucinations are inherent to how LLMs work. Teachers must always verify factual claims, especially for curriculum content.

questions.scenario: A student starts calling their AI chatbot by a name and says, "It really understands me." As their teacher, why is this a concern?

options.label: A
options.text: It isn't - it's a sign of good engagement

options.label: B
options.text: Anthropomorphising AI can lead to over-trust, emotional dependency, and a misunderstanding of what AI actually is - a statistical model, not a sentient being

options.label: C
options.text: It's only a concern if the student is under 13

questions.rationale: DfE guidance explicitly warns against anthropomorphising AI. Teachers should help students understand AI's limitations.

questions.scenario: Which of the following best describes "Narrow AI" versus "Generative AI"?

options.label: A
options.text: Narrow AI can do everything; Generative AI is limited to text

options.label: B
options.text: Narrow AI performs one specific task (e.g., a spam filter); Generative AI can produce a wide range of new content (text, images, code, audio)

options.label: C
options.text: They are different names for the same technology

questions.rationale: Understanding the spectrum of AI helps teachers identify which tools are appropriate for different educational tasks.

questions.scenario: Why is it important for teachers to understand that most AI systems operate as a "black box"?

options.label: A
options.text: Because it means the AI is broken

options.label: B
options.text: Because it means the AI keeps your data secret

options.label: C
options.text: Because the internal reasoning process is not transparent, making it difficult to understand why a specific output was generated - which is critical when using AI for educational decisions

questions.rationale: Black box systems make it impossible to fully audit AI decisions, reinforcing the need for human oversight in education.

sections.title: Part 2: Data Protection and Privacy / UK GDPR

questions.scenario: A teacher uses a free, open AI chatbot to analyse a student's behavioural report by pasting the full text. What is the primary risk?

options.label: A
options.text: The AI might give bad advice

options.label: B
options.text: Free tools often lack enterprise-grade data protection. The student's personal data could be stored, used for training, or breached - violating UK GDPR

options.label: C
options.text: There is no risk if the tool has a privacy policy

questions.rationale: Open tools process data on external servers. Student personal data requires tools with appropriate data processing agreements.

questions.scenario: Under UK GDPR, which "lawful basis" is most commonly used by schools to process student data through AI tools?

options.label: A
options.text: Consent from parents

options.label: B
options.text: The teacher's professional judgment

options.label: C
options.text: "Legitimate interest" or "public task" - but only after a proper Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) has been conducted

questions.rationale: Schools rarely rely on consent alone. DPIAs are essential before introducing AI tools that process personal data.

questions.scenario: What is the key difference between an "Enterprise" AI tool and a "Free/Open" AI tool from a data perspective?

options.label: A
options.text: Enterprise tools are faster

options.label: B
options.text: Enterprise tools typically have contractual data protection agreements, don't train on your data, and comply with organisational policies. Free tools often have weaker or no such guarantees

options.label: C
options.text: There is no meaningful difference

questions.rationale: Enterprise agreements provide legal safeguards. Free tools may use input data for model training.

questions.scenario: Before your school adopts a new AI tool, what should always be completed first?

options.label: A
options.text: A staff training session

options.label: B
options.text: A trial with one class

options.label: C
options.text: A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) to evaluate risks to student and staff data

questions.rationale: DPIAs are a legal requirement under UK GDPR when processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals.

questions.scenario: A school uses an AI tool to flag students who may need intervention based on attendance and grade data. What is the biggest concern?

options.label: A
options.text: The AI might flag too many students

options.label: B
options.text: AI-driven decisions about individuals must have meaningful human oversight. Automated profiling of children without review risks unfair or inaccurate outcomes

options.label: C
options.text: It saves teachers time, so there are no concerns

questions.rationale: DfE guidance is clear that AI should support - not replace - professional judgment, especially in high-stakes decisions about children.

questions.scenario: A student submits an essay. The teacher pastes it into an AI tool for marking. Who owns the intellectual property of that essay?

options.label: A
options.text: The AI tool, because it processed the content

options.label: B
options.text: The school, because it was submitted as schoolwork

options.label: C
options.text: The student (or their parent/guardian), as they are the original creator

questions.rationale: Students retain copyright over their original work. Uploading it to AI tools may violate their IP rights.

questions.scenario: A teacher uses AI to generate a full lesson plan. They share it as their own work. What is the IP risk?

options.label: A
options.text: There is no risk - AI-generated content has no copyright

options.label: B
options.text: The legal status of AI-generated content's copyright is currently unclear and evolving. Teachers should be transparent about AI assistance and check their school's policy

options.label: C
options.text: The AI company owns the copyright

questions.rationale: Copyright law for AI-generated content is unsettled. Schools should have clear policies on attribution and transparency.

questions.scenario: If AI-generated content unknowingly includes material from a copyrighted source, who could be liable?

options.label: A
options.text: Only the AI company

options.label: B
options.text: The person who published or shared the content could be liable for "secondary copyright infringement" even if they didn't know it was copied

options.label: C
options.text: Nobody - AI content is always original

questions.rationale: Users bear responsibility for checking AI output for potential copyright infringement before publishing or sharing.

questions.scenario: A teacher uploads a copyrighted textbook PDF into an AI tool to generate a summary. What should they consider?

options.label: A
options.text: It's fine because it's for educational use

options.label: B
options.text: Uploading copyrighted material to AI tools may breach licensing terms. The "fair dealing" exception for education is narrow and may not cover AI processing

options.label: C
options.text: Copyright doesn't apply to digital content

questions.rationale: Fair dealing exceptions are limited. Teachers should check licensing before uploading copyrighted materials to AI.

questions.scenario: According to DfE guidance, what should always be the top priority when introducing AI in schools?

options.label: A
options.text: Improving exam results

options.label: B
options.text: Reducing teacher workload

options.label: C
options.text: The safety of children - including data safety, emotional safety, and protection from harmful AI-generated content

questions.rationale: Safeguarding is the foundation. All AI adoption must be evaluated through the lens of child safety first.

questions.scenario: What is the safeguarding risk of AI-generated "deepfakes" in a school context?

options.label: A
options.text: They are only a concern for celebrities

options.label: B
options.text: AI can generate realistic fake images, audio, or video of real people - including children. This creates risks of bullying, CSAM (Child Sexual Abuse Material), and reputational harm

options.label: C
options.text: Deepfakes are easy to detect so there is minimal risk

questions.rationale: DfE guidance highlights deepfakes as a serious safeguarding concern. Schools must include this in their online safety policies.

questions.scenario: A student becomes emotionally attached to an AI chatbot companion app and confides personal problems to it instead of trusted adults. Why is this a safeguarding concern?

options.label: A
options.text: It's not - AI can provide good emotional support

options.label: B
options.text: AI cannot provide genuine care, may give harmful advice, and the student may stop seeking help from qualified humans. Data shared may also not be secure

options.label: C
options.text: It's only a problem if the student is very young

questions.rationale: Over-reliance on AI for emotional support can delay appropriate intervention and create data privacy risks.

questions.scenario: Schools must ensure AI tools meet the same standards as other online services. Which framework should guide this?

options.label: A
options.text: The school's social media policy

options.label: B
options.text: Keeping Children Safe in Education (KCSIE) and the school's filtering and monitoring standards

options.label: C
options.text: The AI company's own safety guidelines

questions.rationale: KCSIE is the statutory framework. AI tools must meet the same filtering, monitoring, and safeguarding standards as any other school technology.

questions.scenario: A student receives a convincing AI-generated phishing email that appears to be from their teacher. This is an example of:

options.label: A
options.text: A harmless prank

options.label: B
options.text: AI-enabled social engineering/scam - a growing online safety threat that schools must address in their digital literacy curriculum

options.label: C
options.text: A technical error in the school's email system

questions.rationale: AI-generated scams are increasingly sophisticated. Schools should teach students to verify communications and report suspicious messages.

questions.scenario: A school invests in an "AI detection tool" to catch students using AI in their essays. What should they know?

options.label: A
options.text: These tools are 100% accurate

options.label: B
options.text: Current AI detection tools are unreliable - they produce high rates of false positives (especially for EAL students) and false negatives. They should not be used as sole evidence of misconduct

options.label: C
options.text: They only work for ChatGPT, not other AI tools

questions.rationale: DfE guidance warns against over-reliance on AI detection tools. Professional judgment and process-based assessment are more reliable.

questions.scenario: What is the most effective way to maintain academic integrity in the age of AI?

options.label: A
options.text: Banning all AI use

options.label: B
options.text: Using AI detection software on every submission

options.label: C
options.text: Redesigning assessments to focus on process (drafts, reflections, verbal explanations) rather than solely on the final product, and having clear, transparent policies

questions.rationale: Process-based assessment and clear policies are more effective and equitable than detection-based approaches.

questions.scenario: A student submits work that a teacher suspects was largely AI-generated. What is the appropriate first step?

options.label: A
options.text: Give the student a zero

options.label: B
options.text: Run it through an AI detector and act on the result

options.label: C
options.text: Have a professional conversation with the student, ask them to explain their process and thinking, and refer to the school's academic integrity policy

questions.rationale: Professional dialogue is more reliable than AI detection. Students deserve a fair process before any sanctions.

questions.scenario: Your school's homework policy was written before generative AI became widely available. What should happen?

options.label: A
options.text: It's still fine - homework rules don't change

options.label: B
options.text: Nothing - students won't use AI for homework

options.label: C
options.text: The policy should be reviewed and updated to address how AI may and may not be used for homework, with clear expectations communicated to students and parents

questions.rationale: Policies must evolve with technology. Clear, updated guidance prevents confusion and ensures fairness.

sections.title: Part 6: Meaningful Use, Interacting with AI, and Pedagogy

questions.scenario: "Prompt engineering" is an important skill for teachers using AI. What does it mean?

options.label: A
options.text: Programming the AI's source code

options.label: B
options.text: Crafting clear, specific, and structured instructions to get the best possible output from an AI tool

options.label: C
options.text: Buying a premium subscription for better results

questions.rationale: Good prompts produce better results. Teachers who understand prompt engineering get significantly more value from AI tools.

questions.scenario: You use AI to generate reading comprehension questions. The AI produces questions that assume all students celebrate Christmas. This is an example of:

options.label: A
options.text: A good set of seasonal questions

options.label: B
options.text: Algorithmic bias - where the AI reflects the biases present in its training data, potentially marginalising students from different cultural or religious backgrounds

options.label: C
options.text: A minor formatting issue

questions.rationale: AI bias can inadvertently exclude or stereotype. Teachers must review AI output for cultural sensitivity and inclusivity.

questions.scenario: Which of the following is the LOWEST risk use of AI for a teacher?

options.label: A
options.text: Using AI to generate pastoral reports about named students

options.label: B
options.text: Using AI to draft a generic lesson starter or create a set of example sentences for a grammar exercise

options.label: C
options.text: Using AI to predict which students will fail their GCSEs

questions.rationale: Low-risk uses involve no personal data and allow full teacher review. High-risk uses involve student data and automated decisions.

questions.scenario: A teacher wants to use AI to create differentiated resources for a student with SEND. What must they ensure?

options.label: A
options.text: That the AI tool has a "SEND mode"

options.label: B
options.text: That the AI-generated resources are reviewed by a qualified professional (e.g., SENCo), that no identifiable student data is entered, and that the resources genuinely meet the student's specific needs rather than generic "simplified" content

options.label: C
options.text: Nothing extra - AI automatically adapts to SEND needs

questions.rationale: AI cannot understand individual SEND needs without proper context. Human expertise must validate all SEND resources.

questions.scenario: The principle of "Human in the Loop" means:

options.label: A
options.text: A human built the AI, so it's always trustworthy

options.label: B
options.text: A qualified human must review, validate, and take responsibility for any AI-generated output before it is used with students or for decision-making

options.label: C
options.text: The AI is checking the human's work

questions.rationale: This is a core DfE principle. AI assists; humans decide. No AI output should be used without professional review.

questions.scenario: A teacher asks AI to generate a 10-question quiz from a textbook chapter. Before using it in class, they should:

options.label: A
options.text: Print it immediately to save time

options.label: B
options.text: Check every question for accuracy, ensure the difficulty is appropriate, verify it aligns with learning objectives, and confirm no copyrighted content has been reproduced verbatim

options.label: C
options.text: Only check the first and last questions

questions.rationale: Quality assurance is non-negotiable. Every AI-generated resource must be fully reviewed before classroom use.

questions.scenario: A colleague argues that using AI is "wasteful" because of its environmental impact (energy, water, carbon). How should you respond?

options.label: A
options.text: Ignore them - technology always has a cost

options.label: B
options.text: They raise a valid point. AI tools consume significant computational resources. Teachers should use AI purposefully and proportionately rather than habitually, and schools should consider the environmental impact in their AI policies

options.label: C
options.text: AI is carbon neutral

questions.rationale: Environmental awareness is part of responsible AI use. Purposeful, targeted use minimises unnecessary computational waste.
